title: CLI mesh-smoke-test command
description: An automation CLI command 'uv run ixdar-cli mesh-smoke-test' that: (1) checks if the app is running via health endpoint, (2) triggers DSL graph execution at default parameters via the automation HTTP endpoint, (3) polls mesh-state for completion, (4) asserts vertex count within expected range and response time < threshold, (5) reports pass/fail as structured JSON. This gives agents and developers a single command to verify that the mesh pipeline works at production parameters without needing to manually launch and inspect the viewer.
definition-of-done: 1. 'uv run ixdar-cli mesh-smoke-test' runs the full pipeline and returns JSON with pass/fail, vertex count, face count, and elapsed time. 2. Exit code is 0 on pass, 1 on fail. 3. Failure reasons include: app not running, timeout, OOM (detected via error response), vertex count out of range. 4. The command can be run by an agent as a verification step.
testing-plan: 1. Launch the mesh viewer. Run mesh-smoke-test, verify pass. 2. Without the app running, run mesh-smoke-test, verify it reports 'app not running'. 3. Modify the DSL to produce a degenerate mesh, run mesh-smoke-test, verify it reports failure.
todos: Add mesh-smoke-test command to Python CLI
Implement health check, graph execution trigger, state polling, and assertions
Define expected vertex count range for tool_quilt.dsl at default params
Define timeout threshold (e.g., 30s for graph execution)
Return structured JSON result with timing and mesh stats
unknowns: Whether the automation API already supports triggering DSL execution or if a new endpoint is needed
Whether to hardcode the expected vertex count range or compute it from subdivision level
